```
myorigin = /etc/mailname

mailbox_size_limit = 0
recipient_delimiter = +
inet_interfaces = all

```

Then I put sudo nano /etc/postfix/virtual and here put:
```
user@vpc2a.wi.zut.edu.pl user

```

Next run command:
sudo postmap /etc/postfix/virtual

sudo service postfix restart

### Post by TheFu on 2014-12-18
Perhaps if you explain what you are trying to accomplish?

Running an email server on the internet requires public DNS records know about your domain and server with DNS A, IN and MX records.
I tried to find your server and couldn't. That means the DNS isn't setup correctly and if you are not the network admin for the school, it may be either hard or impossible to run a mail server there. OTOH, if you are the email admin for that domain, add the DNS records as needed. Until gmail.com can see your server, email from them will not flow.

There are other ways to do this leveraging another cheap server on the internet, but that is difficult for someone just learning mail servers to setup.

It is also possible to setup a local email server that acts as clearing house for other, public, email accounts at other services. There are a few people here using their gmail account in that way, but I've never done this.  For me, using someone elses' email server defeats the purpose of running an email server myself.

---

### Post by TheFu on 2014-12-19
> **Micha_Sabat said:**
> OK.

How should I organize my DNS records.

Or mayby I could only create local mail serwer for my wirtual users?
How to do that?

You need public DNS for the server so that anyone in the world can find it by the domain name you specify. Then you just need to add an MX DNS record so that other email servers know to send email for the domain to that specific machine. Most networks have an existing DNS solution, so just use that.

If you are the network admin, then you should know how this is currently done and who to contact to make it so. Don't forget that inbound and outbound ports to the server for SMTP need to be open (not firewalled). On most networks, this will require edge router and firewall changes. Contact that team to get it done, approvals are usually necessary for any org over 200 people.

Without DNS, email will be limited to user accounts on the machine (localhost) and almost every other internet email server will reject any attempts to send messages.

Howtoforge.com has step-by-step instructions to set this stuff up, as do many other websites. [https://help.ubuntu.com/14.04/serverguide/dns.html](https://help.ubuntu.com/14.04/serverguide/dns.html) and [https://help.ubuntu.com/14.04/serverguide/email-services.html](https://help.ubuntu.com/14.04/serverguide/email-services.html) are the official Ubuntu docs. Have you seen those?

---
